issues.title: Need to define a passive device
issues.author: italobusi
issues.body: Further investigate whether there is a need to define a passive device list or whether passive devices can be modelled using the network-element list of the base inventory model and defining a new ne-type

comments.author: mamali68
comments.body: i think to answer this we need to identify the differences of "A physical network element" versus "A passive network element" and if identity ne-type can be extended to be applied to a passive network element.

issues.title: Need to associate a link with a cable
issues.author: italobusi
issues.body:
It is not clear whether there is a need to define some leafref to report the association between a link (in the topology model) and a cable (in the passive inventory model)

The following picture tries to explain how this association can be derived from existing information in the topology inventory model (associating a TP with a port component) and the base and passive inventory models:

[link-cable-relationship-00.pptx](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18007659/link-cable-relationship-00.pptx)

See: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/inventory-yang/MxdA8RUlwuRnIbLKfgR_NFZRB0Y/

comments.author: nigel-r-davis
comments.body:
As you are aware, in TAPI we concluded that we needed to add a relationship from the link in the functional/logical topology to the passive inventory. BUT we chose to provide a sliding abstraction where the model could be very shallow providing just "abstract strands" between relevant passive devices or could provide a deeper model where the abstract strand is a 1:1  to the actual strand.

So, to the question, yes we do need to provide the opportunity to relate the functional/logical link to the passive inventory model. This should be a conditional opportunity (augment) and it should provide various alternative abstractions.

issues.author: italobusi

issues.title: Identities clean-up, passive-device-types
issues.author: mamali68
issues.body:
The Identities defined can mostly be changed to typedef with a enumeration that addresses the types. 
Another suggestion is the base identities and their use-cases be moved to a types yang file to reduce the size of the main module. i.e ietf-nwi-passive-device-types.yang. 

Or we reduce the identities to only one base identity namely passive-device and nest it, as most of these identities share same attributes in reality such as length, attenuation,..

issues.title: Modeling intra-NE cables and passive devices
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body: There are passive devices, e.g. filter, splitter, fixed attenuator, and fiber cables that connect cards/ports in the same NE. Can they be modeled as inventory in the same way as external cables and passive devices?

issues.title: Modeling methodology for passive inventory
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
There are different thoughts regarding how passive devices and cables can be modeled, i.e.,
- modeled as new inventory objects (as -v0 of this draft defines)
- modeled as new NE types for passive devices, and new inventory object for cables
- modeled as topology objects by augmenting RFC8345, also be used as part of SIMAP hierarchy

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body:
Checking the differences between NEs and passive device, I have found:

The following attributes are in common (or adapted to be in common):

| network-element | passive-device
|---|---
| ne-id | id
| uuid | uuid
| name | name
| description | description
| alias | alias
| ne-type | device-type

The custom-tags attribute defined for the passive-device looks quite generic and applicable also to managed network elements

The location-ref defined for the passive-device can be redefined like for the active network element by a reference for the location inventory to the network-element

The following network-element attributes are not defined for the passive devices but it is not clear why they are not applicable to passive devices as well:
- hardware-rev (to be renamed is as product-rev)
- product-name

The following network-element attributes are planned to be removed also from the base inventory model:
- mfg-date
- serial-number

The following network-element attributes are not applicable to passive devices but are currently under discussion whether to keep or removed and optional since not applicable to all the managed network element instances:
- software-rev
- software-patch-rev*

issues.title: Scope of passive inventory
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
Define the scope of passive inventory management. E.g.:

- Type of network and devices
- Type of cables and passive sites
- Intra- and/or inter-NE devices

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body: For the cables, see also some comments in https://github.com/ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang/issues/29#issuecomment-2607717141

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
Input:

Passive components: functional components that are undetectable, inside & outside a box (NE?)
TAPI representation: 
 - span between connectors (not between the pins of connectors). span contains strands, span can be multi-ended. span is a functional component. If RFC8345 link is extended to support mp2mp links it is equivalent to a span 
 - joint where multiple fibers can be combined

TBD: to clarify the rationale of modeling
weekly calls

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body:
> * span between connectors (not between the pins of connectors). span contains strands, span can be multi-ended. span is a functional component. If RFC8345 link is extended to support mp2mp links it is equivalent to a span.

I understand that if the span is p2p, we can model it as a link using the Network Inventory Topology model in [draft-ietf-ivy-network-inventory-topology](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-ivy-network-inventory-topology), which augments RFC8345.

IMHO, p2p spans are the typical spans being used in optical transport networks.

I would like to see some examples of mp2mp spans to validate whether this approach can be extended or there is a need to model the span explicitly in the network inventory model (base or augmentations, like the passive inventory).

As already discussed in NMOP WG, RFC8345 can model mp2mp links. The debate is whether the current model is "good enough" or needs to be re-designed.

See also some notes from previous discussion: https://github.com/ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang/issues/32#issuecomment-2075971592

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
For discussion - passive infrastructure based on Italo's previous slides.

[passive-infrastructure.pptx](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18625461/passive-infrastructure.pptx)

comments.author: sergiobelotti
comments.body:
> > * span between connectors (not between the pins of connectors). span contains strands, span can be multi-ended. span is a functional component. If RFC8345 link is extended to support mp2mp links it is equivalent to a span.
> 
> I understand that if the span is p2p, we can model it as a link using the Network Inventory Topology model in [draft-ietf-ivy-network-inventory-topology](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-ivy-network-inventory-topology), which augments RFC8345.
> 
> IMHO, p2p spans are the typical spans being used in optical transport networks.
> 
> I would like to see some examples of mp2mp spans to validate whether this approach can be extended or there is a need to model the span explicitly in the network inventory model (base or augmentations, like the passive inventory).
> 
> As already discussed in NMOP WG, RFC8345 can model mp2mp links. The debate is whether the current model is "good enough" or needs to be re-designed.
> 
> See also some notes from previous discussion: [ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang#32 (comment)](https://github.com/ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang/issues/32#issuecomment-2075971592)

drfat-ietf-ivy-netowrk-inventory-topology, is augmenting termination-point (TP) of RFC8345 referencing a "port" as inventory element from which TP is abstracted, and is augmenting node of RFC8345 referencing NE as inventory element from which node is abstracted. Following the same path, what would be the inventory element from which "link" is abstracted ? The span?

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body: These are some slides that I have drafted during the **[2024-04-24 Network Inventory weekly call](https://github.com/ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang/blob/main/minutes/minutes-2024-04-24.md)** but not uploaded on github: [physical-span-model-00.pptx](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18644053/physical-span-model-00.pptx)

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body:
> > > * span between connectors (not between the pins of connectors). span contains strands, span can be multi-ended. span is a functional component. If RFC8345 link is extended to support mp2mp links it is equivalent to a span.
> > 
> > 
> > I understand that if the span is p2p, we can model it as a link using the Network Inventory Topology model in [draft-ietf-ivy-network-inventory-topology](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-ivy-network-inventory-topology), which augments RFC8345.
> > IMHO, p2p spans are the typical spans being used in optical transport networks.
> > I would like to see some examples of mp2mp spans to validate whether this approach can be extended or there is a need to model the span explicitly in the network inventory model (base or augmentations, like the passive inventory).
> > As already discussed in NMOP WG, RFC8345 can model mp2mp links. The debate is whether the current model is "good enough" or needs to be re-designed.
> > See also some notes from previous discussion: [ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang#32 (comment)](https://github.com/ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang/issues/32#issuecomment-2075971592)
> 
> drfat-ietf-ivy-netowrk-inventory-topology, is augmenting termination-point (TP) of RFC8345 referencing a "port" as inventory element from which TP is abstracted, and is augmenting node of RFC8345 referencing NE as inventory element from which node is abstracted. Following the same path, what would be the inventory element from which "link" is abstracted ? The span?

That was my initially thought but after some discussion on the list I am not sure we need to directly associate the link with a span, see https://github.com/aguoietf/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory/issues/2

Specific attributes of these devices should be identified to be included in the MW model in some way (may be Radio capabilities or Radio Equipment Inventory indicated these attributes).

issues.title: add feature to leaf fiber-core-num
issues.author: mamali68
issues.body:
the leaf **fiber core-num** is making the grouping **passive-device-ports** specific to optical fiber, 
Suggestion to introduce feature to check if the device supports optical before creating the leaf, 

example:  `feature optical-device ` and check it in the grouping by `if-feature` statement. 

therefore, the same grouping can be used for any other ports.

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body:
IMHO, the feature is too much coarse grained

If a controller have a mixed of optical and non-optical cables, the feature shall be supported but the fiber core-num would be applicable only to optical cables and not to non-optical cables

If there is an attribute indicated whether the cable is optical or non-optical, a when statement seems better to me

Another option could be to generalize the attribute to report either the number of fiber cores or the number of wires in a cable

issues.title: Tom's comments on PON passive infrastructure
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
1)”The methods for discovering these devices are implementation-specific and fall
   outside the scope of this document.”

Tom comment 0: “discovering” may not be the right word here… it is more about ingestion into the (passive) inventory data model, no?

Proposal: "learning about"


2)
“Passive Infrastructure in Optical Access Networks (keep original title)
Proposal: add text to indicate using PON as an example of optical access network with significant passive infrastructure.

   _The_ Passive infrastructure in _passive_ optical networks, often referred to
   as Optical Distribution Network (ODN), is the physical _optical_ fiber-_based_ 
   ~~optic~~ network that connects the Optical Line Terminal (OLT) _typically hosted_ in _a_ ~~the~~
   central office to  with the Optical Network Unit/Terminal (ONU/ONT) _typically deployed_ at the
   user's location.  _The_ ODN ~~uses~~ _is equipped with_ one or multiple (typically two stages) cascaded passive optical
   splitters _thus creating a physical point-to-multipoint fiber network between an OLT port and the multiple connected ONU/ONTs_. ~~to divide the signal from a single OLT into multiple paths to serve multiple users using a single fiber.~~

_The feeder segment of an ODN refers to the cabling between the OLT and the first splitter, whereas the distribution segment of the ODN comprises the fiber cabling between the first and second splitter stage. The drop segment comprises the drop fibers between the ONT/ONU and the second splitter stage._



_The PON_ ODN _hence_ comprises optical
   fiber cables _and splitters but also_ ~~connectors, and~~ many auxiliary components, _such as connectors, fiber distribution frames, wavelength co-existence elements etc.._ .  

_The passive components where the optical signals entering or leaving the optical fibers are split/combined or cross-connected are typically hosted in mini cabinets e.g. at street corners , manholes, attached to utility poles etc…_

Proposal: add also FDT and FAT as examples

~~There are
   multiple types of fibers, including feeder fibers (connecting the OLT
   to a fiber distribution point (FDT)), distribution fibers (connecting
   the distribution point to access points), and drop fibers (connecting
   access points to ONUs/ONTs).~~

(Tom comment 1: above are not really different “types” of fiber; feeder/distribution/drop typically refer to different ODN segments, and typically the cable in these segments will differ in terms of number of cores..)
(tom comment 2: I am not sure “access point” is a commonly used term.. I think it might have its origin from a wholesale-retail perspective?)


~~The ODN cabinet acts as a central point
   where different fiber optic cables and components are hosted, and
   these ODN cabinets are often located on street corners, utility
   poles, or other accessible areas along the route to the end users.~~

   Figure 2 illustrates a typical passive infrastructure for optical
   access networks.”


(Tom comment 3: we discussed on previous call corrections for this figure)


3) 
“Passive device: refers to a physical device within a network that
      does not require external power to function, and simply
      manipulates signals through processes like transmission,
      reflection, splitting, filtering, or attenuation without actively
      amplifying or generating the signal.  Examples include optical
      fibers, splitters, couplers, and optical filters, all of which are
      used to direct and manage ~~light~~ _communication_ signals within a system without
      needing electricity.  A passive device typically does not have
      management interfaces and is typically deployed in a location
      tracked by the network operator.”

Tom comment 4: per above edit, suggest to make the definition applicable to both electrical and optical systems..

Proposal: ...used to direct signals within a system without needing power

issues.title: rename nwi:common-entity-attributes to nwi:basic-common-entity-attributes
issues.author: mamali68
issues.body:
The grouping `common-entity-attributes` does not exist anymore in the base model. it has been broken down into two groupings. the one containing common attributes has been renamed in base model to `basic-common-entity-attributes` by [THIS PULL REQUEST](https://github.com/ietf-ivy-wg/network-inventory-yang/pull/107)
passive inventory shall adopt this change.

issues.title: cable-type to be moved inside grouping common-cable-attributes
issues.author: mamali68
issues.body:
A suggestion to assume `leaf cable-type` as a common-cable-attribute and hence moving it inside the grouping. 
this provides a more comprehensive grouping as the attribute can be used as common and the  change does not affect the module Tree.

issues.title: Add definition for passive inventory
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
Proposed definition:

Passive inventory contains a collection of inventory objects pertaining to network infrastructure that are not powered and have no direct management communication, and that are used to realize the underlying physical infrastructure carrying signals through a network. Passive inventory includes elements such as optical cables, fiber segments, splice points, closures, ducts, and supporting structures that do not perform active processing, forwarding, or control functions. These objects are maintained for purposes such as identification, location, connectivity, and lifecycle management.

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
Meeting on 04/08/2026:

Agree with the definition in general, and note that there are cases where a passive device may be considered as powered, or may be unused (not carrying signal), but can still be modeled by the passive inventory.
Agreed to add the following note to the definition of passive inventory:

(add a non-exhaustive list of conscious exceptions)
- Add a note to indicate that in the case of e.g. raman amplifier where a fiber is performing amplification stimulated by an external pump laser, that fiber is still considered as a passive device.
- Another exception is e.g. a splitter embedded in an active device
- Fibers that are not lit/used

issues.title: modelling of the MPO/MPT cable
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
Another use case to consider is the modelling of the MPO/MPT cable:

https://majorcustomcable.com/product/cables/fiber-connectivity/mtp-mpo-cables/

This can be modelled as a multipoint cable with one A-end and multiple Z-ends. The numbering of the fibers within the cable need to be consistent in the A-end and Z-ends.

issues.title: modeling a fiber with splitter along the fiber
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
A different scenario is where there is a splitter along the fiber:

https://ecatalog.corning.com/optical-communications/AU/en/Fiber-Optic-Hardware/Terminals/OptiSheath%C2%AE-MultiPort-Splitter-Stubless-Terminal/p/optisheath-multiport-splitter-stubless-terminal

https://ecatalog.corning.com/optical-communications/AU/en/Fiber-Optic-Hardware/Terminals/OptiSheath%C2%AE-MultiPort-Splitter-Stubless-Terminal/p/optisheath-multiport-splitter-stubless-terminal

https://www.alldataresource.com/CommScope-CC8312-000-OCC1P-Wideband-Splitter-planar-singlemode-1-x-32-symmetrical-split-ratio-0-250-mm-cable-field-installable_p_601802.html

https://www.commscope.com/product-type/hubs-closures-terminals-boxes/access-terminals/fiber-terminals-hardened/itemmst-0nrh00-a0500u/

https://ecatalog.corning.com/optical-communications/AU/en/Fiber-Optic-Hardware/Terminals/OptiSheath%C2%AE-MultiPort-Flex-Splitter-Terminal/p/optisheath-multiport-flex-splitter-terminal

issues.title: modeling fiber closures
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
There was some discussion about the "fiber closures" which can contain multiple cables. Examples are available at the following URLs:

https://raycom.cz/templates/it-technologie/images/eye_products/87/pdf/cz/datasheet-and-ordering-guide-tenio-fiber-closures.pdf

https://www.commscope.com/product-type/hubs-closures-terminals-boxes/splice-closures/fiber-splice-closures/tenio/

https://www.alcadon.com/PDF/PDF2019/CW6618-000_Product_specifications.pdf

The "fiber closures" can be modelled as passive component within an active NE or passive device. The A-end/Z-end of the cables contained within the "fiber closures" are port components which do not belong to this component.

issues.title: Do we need an ad-hoc model for cable joint box
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body: should we model any passive device as NEs (including cable joint boxes) or only some passive devices and have an ad-hoc model for cable joint boxes

To validate if the model covers the use case of an OCS switch, e.g. 
https://www.lumentum.com/en/blog/optical-circuit-switches-in-ai-hyperscale-data-centers

pulls.title: Clean up repository, YANG code and I-D text
pulls.author: italobusi
pulls.body:
The main branch has been directly updated to use the new template from Med:

https://github.com/IETF-OPS-AD/I-D-with-yang-template

These changes cannot be done using PR but you can check the diff between the I-D generated from the main branch and the latest I-D submitted to IETF from the README of this repository or using this link:

https://author-tools.ietf.org/api/iddiff?doc_1=draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory&url_2=https://aguoietf.github.io/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory.txt

This PR is proposing some clean-up in the repository structure, YANG data model and I-D text (fixing some idnits and aligning also with the guidelines in RFC 9907, formerly known as RFC 8407-bis)

You can check the diff between the I-D generated from this PR branch and the I-D generated from the main branch from the README of this repository or using this link:

https://author-tools.ietf.org/api/iddiff?url_1=https://aguoietf.github.io/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory.txt&url_2=https://aguoietf.github.io/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory/clean-up/draft-ygb-ivy-passive-network-inventory.txt

comments.author: bradspau
comments.body: Question from the meeting of how do you determine how many cables can be present on a physical port the answer is it is a combination of what seals do the enclosures support and the size of the cables. Standard cable sizes compared to physical port sizing plus can you seal that combination of cables.
